— parent and teacher, ages 6–12 — AAP 2019 recommendation.
complex CDDP and comorbidity assessment (6–18 years).
(Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale, WHO/Kessler R.C.) — 18 questions; brief screening in adult patients.
(Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in adults) — structured interview.
(Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function) — executive function.
No specific laboratory indication for routine DDHP. Based on clinical suspicion:
Complete blood count, ferritin (iron deficiency), thyroid function (TSH).
Lead level – if risk factors exist.
Toxicology screening — suspicion of substance use in adolescents.
Before stimulant initiation—ECG only if cardiac risk history or family history (AAP, AHA 2008; routine ECG not required).
EEG — only if seizure suspected or atypical clinical manifestations.
Brain MRI — only for focal neurological signs. No routine indication.
Neurophysiological tests for diagnosis (TOVA, IVA, QbTest) lack sufficient evidence base
The clinical diagnosis and standardized scales are prioritized as the gold standard.
8.1 General Principles (NICE NG87 · AAP 2019 · CADDRA 2018 Consensus)
(PCIT, Triple P, Incredible Years); second-line methylphenidate.

superior to monotherapy or behavioral intervention alone (MTA Cooperative Group. Arch Gen Psychiatry 1999, n=579 — combination superior to monotherapy, but pharmacotherapy stronger than behavioral intervention alone).
— First-line pharmacotherapy (Cortese S. et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2018 network meta-analysis; in children methylphenidate first choice, in adults amphetamine more effective).
Atomoxetine, guanfacine (long-acting formulation), clonidine (long-acting formulation). Used when stimulant contraindicated or side effect present.
— parent training (PCIT, parent management training), school-based intervention (class modifications, behavior contract), child CBT.
— Individualized education plan (IEP / 504 Plan US; EHCP UK); extra time, limited distractors, written vs oral response options.
— Height and weight, blood pressure, heart rate (3-month), sleep, appetite, mood. In stimulants, slight decrease in height growth (1–2 cm with long-term use) is expected.

Anxiety/depression (SSRI + CBT), tics (alpha-2 agonists or cautiously stimulants), substance use (lisdexamfetamine or atomoxetine — lower abuse potential), autism (atypical antipsychotic for comorbid irritability).
Once-daily dose; convenient for school day.
Low abuse potential (prodrug); predominant in adults.
Effect 4–6 weeks; slight increase in risk of suicidal ideation (FDA black box).
Sedation, hypotension; beneficial in comorbid tic cases.
Sedation; beneficial when sleep disorder is comorbid.
5+ years — environmental changes first; then behavioral or pharmacotherapy. In adults — first-line stimulant; second-line atomoxetine.
Cortese S. et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2018 (network meta-analiz):
children — methylphenidate first-line; adults — amphetamine first-line (higher effect size); atomoxetine moderately effective.
MTA Cooperative Group (Arch Gen Psychiatry 1999, follow-up Hechtman L. et al. JAACAP 2016):
intensive pharmacotherapy combination demonstrated superiority in 14-month observation; but differences weaken at 36-month and 8-year follow-up — long-term effect debated.
Monitoring height and weight, baseline cardiovascular parameters.

For children aged 4–7 years; two phases – Child-Directed Interaction (CDI) and Parent-Directed Interaction (PDI). Development of parental behavior management skills with real-time therapist coaching (in-vivo coaching).
Parent, teacher, and child programs; group format 14–22 sessions; ASTPS model (Authoritative Stimulating Trustworthy Parenting Style).
Triple P — Positive Parenting Program — Sanders (Sanders M.)
The 5-tier system developed in Australia is universal, selective, targeted, and individual. Effective in numerous RCTs.
8–12 sessions behavior principles-based parent training – token system, time-out, continuous training.
CBT for adult ADHD (Safren CBT for Adult ADHD) — Safren (Safren S.A.)
CBT modules for adult ADHD — task organization, planning, distraction management, cognitive restructuring. Evidence: Safren S.A. et al. JAMA 2010 RCT.
Recommended by AAP 2019; parent and teacher forms, comorbidity subscales.
Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS) — Kessler (Kessler R.C.), WHO
18 questions; short 6-item screening section. Clinical and population screening in adult patients.
Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults (DIVA-5 — Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults)
Structured interview for adult ADHD — according to DSM-5 criteria.
6–18 years comprehensive assessment – DHDD symptoms, executive function, behavioral disorder, learning.
Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function (BRIEF-2 — Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function)
— Parent and teacher assessment of executive function — inhibition, working memory, emotional regulation.

In ADHD, neurofeedback training targeting specific brainwave patterns (theta/beta ratio). Evidence: Holtmann M. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2011 meta-analysis — small effect size; effect debated among field specialists. NICE NG87 — not first-line, adjunct status.
